excerpt: The taʾwīl of Sūrat al-Takāthur: the visit to the graves cannot be a forced removal — for ziyāra is a meeting freely undertaken, from which the visitor returns. The true ziyāra is the voluntary spiritual death enjoined by the Prophet's saying 'Die before you die,' through which the believer attaches himself to the Friend of God and so reaches the grave that is a garden of Paradise. The naʿīm about which God shall ask is the wisdom flowing from the tongues of the Prophets and the Friends of God — and in its highest sense, Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī and the Imāms of his line are themselves al-naʿīm.
source_authority: al-Muʾayyad fī al-Dīn al-Shīrāzī (d. 470/1078), Fatimid dāʿī al-duʿāt under Imām-Caliph al-Mustanṣir

excerpt: The taʾwīl of the oaths in Sūrat al-Tīn, transmitted from Mawlānā Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq to a questioning companion: the fig signifies Ādam — and beneath that, the Qāʾim — for he came forth without antecedent as the fig appears directly upon the branch; the olive signifies Nūḥ — and beneath that, Ibrāhīm — for the olive's true fruit is its oil, which spread farther than the fruit itself; Mount Sinai signifies Mūsā — and beneath that, ʿĪsā, the lineage that issued from Sinai; the Trustworthy Land signifies the Prophet Muḥammad and, hidden within him, the Qāʾim of his House. These oaths were veiled in cipher so that the true sciences would not reach the unworthy, but only those of insight.
source_authority: al-Muʾayyad fī al-Dīn al-Shīrāzī (d. 470/1078), Fatimid dāʿī al-duʿāt under Imām-Caliph al-Mustanṣir

excerpt: Al-Kirmani lays out the graded order of the hudud al-din — Imam, Bab, Hujja, Da'i, Ma'dhun, Mustajib — as the earthly counterpart of the cosmic hierarchy of intellects, each rank receiving from the limit above (al-hadd al-'ali) and conveying to the limit below (al-hadd al-dani).
source_location_hint: Discussion of the hudud al-din and their correspondence to the cosmic order

excerpt: Al-Sijistani treats the offices of the da'wa — the Da'i, the Ma'dhun, and the Mustajib — as ordered ranks through which the seeker is initiated, each office discharging a distinct function in the conveyance of esoteric knowledge.
source_location_hint: On the ranks of the da'wa

excerpt: In the ta'wil of Surat al-Qadr, Laylat al-Qadr signifies Fatima al-Zahra' and al-Qadr signifies the Prophet Muhammad, her father; the Sura thereby alludes to the spiritual descent of prophetic light into her person and through her into the line of the Imams.

Nasir-i Khusraw reads the descent of the angels and the Spirit on Laylat al-Qadr as the perpetual transmission of inner knowledge from the highest rank downward through the ranks of the da'wa, continuing 'until the breaking of dawn,' which signifies the manifestation of the Qa'im.
